frame_title: The Agony and Ecstasy
current_data_result_claims: Long-run index returns are driven by a few high-performing stocks, while a considerable share of constituents suffer catastrophic declines. | Some constituents experience deep and persistent drawdowns without recovery. | Passive strategies may hold imploding firms for too long.
rationale: Core narrative can likely be retained with minor wording refresh.

frame_title: Research Question
current_data_result_claims: To what extent does a ``Crash-Filtered'' equity index, constructed via probabilistic implosion modeling, generate superior risk-adjusted returns compared to the market benchmark and traditional minimum-volatility strategies? | The probabilistic implosion model follows the Catastrophic Stock Implosion approach proposed by Tewari et al. (2024). Instead of relying only on formal bankruptcy declarations, CSI uses market-based price-path signals. | How does the integration of Autoencoders for feature engineering impact the Average Precision of Ensemble models compared to those trained solely on raw financial data?
rationale: Core narrative can likely be retained with minor wording refresh.

frame_title: Dataset
current_data_result_claims: The main data sources are WRDS and FRED. The OOS index testing is kept strictly seperated from the training data for walk-forward CV and the test set. frame_title: Methodology I: Response Variable Classification
current_data_result_claims: Following Tewari et al. (2024), a stock is classified as a CSI ( y=1 ) if it satisfies all three criteria: | Initial Crash ( C ): more than a C drawdown from the trailing peak marks the beginning of the ``zombie'' period. warningRed C -80 | Non-Recovery ( M ): maximum cumulative return remains below the recovery ceiling M during the zombie period. warningRed M -20
rationale: Likely needs revised June data/results refresh.

frame_title: Applying the classification to the CRSP dataset
current_data_result_claims: frame Applying the classification to the CRSP dataset | The current cleaned scaffold keeps only observable CRSP firm-years. frame_title: Methodology II: ``Temporary-CSI''
current_data_result_claims: frame Methodology II: ``Temporary-CSI'' | Original CSI rule unchanged. frame_title: Robustness II: Do CSI-firms recover again?
current_data_result_claims: frame Robustness II: Do CSI-firms recover again? | CSI flags mostly identify persistent declines; recovery risk appears mainly in with a smaller drawdown threshhold | After a temporary-CSI flag, the robustness grid tracks the five-year recovery bucket across alternative crash/recovery windows.

frame_title: Methodology III: ``Permanent-CSI''
current_data_result_claims: frame Methodology III: ``Permanent-CSI'' | alertblock Dealing with recovering firms | Permanent-CSI starts from a confirmed temporary-CSI event and separates terminal failure, durable non-recovery, resolved recovery, and unresolved PCL windows.

frame_title: Index Construction I: From Score to Portfolio Weight
current_data_result_claims: frame Index Construction I: From Score to Portfolio Weight | The model produces a probability of CSI next year. A threshold turns that probability into a binary exclusion flag. The flag is then applied to market-cap weights. Two regimes differ only in how long the flag persists after it first fires. | From model score to portfolio weight
